title: Targeting the Poor: Evidence from a Field Experiment in Indonesia
authors: Vivi Alatas, Abhijit Banerjee, Rema Hanna, Benjamin A. Olken, Julia Tobias
methodology: Randomized controlled trial (RCT) across 640 villages in North Sumatra and South Sulawesi, Indonesia. Villages were randomly assigned to one of three targeting arms: (1) community-based targeting (CBT) via town-hall meetings where residents rank neighbours by poverty; (2) proxy means test (PMT) using household survey data and statistical scoring; (3) a hybrid approach combining PMT lists with community verification meetings.
data_used: Household survey data from approximately 16,000 households across 640 villages in Indonesia (2008). Data collected on consumption, assets, and demographic characteristics to benchmark targeting accuracy.
key_findings: CBT better identifies households that communities consider poor; PMT better tracks per-capita consumption expenditure. CBT has lower administrative costs and higher community legitimacy. The hybrid approach performs well on both metrics. Targeting errors exist under all methods. Elite households are somewhat over-represented in CBT lists but the effect is modest. Community and consumption-based definitions of poverty diverge significantly — raising the normative question of whose poverty definition should govern program eligibility.
gap_addressed: The literature lacked rigorous experimental evidence on which targeting mechanism — CBT or PMT — actually identifies the poor better in practice, and at what cost. Existing comparisons were non-experimental and confounded by program context.
gaps_highlighted: Results are from Indonesia and may not generalise to weaker-state, higher-poverty contexts. The paper does not resolve whether community or consumption-based definitions of poverty are normatively correct. Elite capture mechanisms are not directly tested. Long-run effects of targeting choices on social cohesion are not examined. The study assumes sufficient within-village inequality to make discrimination feasible.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: This is the anchor CBT paper, but its implicit assumption — that meaningful discrimination between poor and non-poor is socially and statistically feasible — breaks down in Mali's Sahel, where near-universal vulnerability means selection ratios are so low that community committees face not an information problem but a social impossibility problem. The 'managing the impossible' dynamic is precisely what this paper's design cannot capture.

title: Does Elite Capture Matter? Local Elites and Targeted Welfare Programs in Indonesia
authors: Vivi Alatas, Abhijit Banerjee, Rema Hanna, Benjamin A. Olken, Ririn Purnamasari, Matthew Wai-Poi
methodology: Combined field experiment and non-experimental analysis. In 400 villages, the researchers experimentally varied the degree of elite influence over targeting decisions for the PKH conditional cash transfer program. The non-experimental component analysed allocation patterns across multiple existing Indonesian transfer programs (PKH, Raskin, BLT) using household survey and administrative data. Elite status was defined both by formal leadership positions and informal community standing.
data_used: Data from 400 villages in Indonesia. Household-level survey data merged with administrative records from multiple transfer programs (PKH, Raskin, BLT). The 2008 PPLS (poverty pre-screening) household census provided consumption benchmarks. Elite identification drew on village leadership rosters and kinship data.
key_findings: Conditional on consumption, there is overall little evidence that elites and their relatives are disproportionately selected as beneficiaries. However, this masks important heterogeneity: formal leaders (e.g. village heads) are more likely to receive benefits, while informal leaders are less likely. Capture occurs at the benefit distribution stage, not the list-making stage. Critically, the welfare losses from elite capture are quantitatively small — eliminating capture entirely would improve program welfare gains by less than 1% — because formal elites are only about 9% richer than non-elites and represent at most 15% of the population.
gap_addressed: Elite capture of CBT programs had been a major theoretical concern but lacked rigorous empirical quantification. The paper tests whether the concern translates into meaningful welfare losses in practice.
gaps_highlighted: Results may not generalise to contexts where elites are substantially wealthier relative to the poor. The study cannot detect subtler forms of capture (e.g. pressure, exclusion of marginalised groups). It does not directly test CBT meeting dynamics. Social pressure from non-elites — a different political economy problem — is not examined. The paper focuses on Indonesia's relatively institutionalised village governance.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: The paper's most striking finding for Mali is that elite capture matters little precisely because elites are barely better off than non-elites in the Indonesian study villages. This logic extends — and intensifies — in Mali's Sahel, where near-universal poverty means there is virtually no consumption gradient between 'elites' and 'the poor' to exploit. The committee dilemma is therefore not classic elite capture but something more structurally intractable: when everyone qualifies on welfare grounds, no legitimate selection principle exists.

title: Community-Based Targeting Mechanisms for Social Safety Nets: A Critical Review
methodology: Theoretical review using a principal-agent framework, synthesising case study evidence and existing empirical literature on community involvement in beneficiary selection and benefit delivery for social safety nets. No primary data collected. The authors develop a typology of community agents and derive conditions under which CBT is likely to outperform or underperform centralised alternatives.
data_used: No primary data. Draws on case studies and program evaluations from multiple countries, including India's Integrated Rural Development Project, World Bank country surveys (Subbarao et al., 1997), and a range of NGO-implemented safety net programs across Africa, Asia, and Latin America.
key_findings: Community participation in targeting can improve outcomes by exploiting local information and social capital, but gains are systematically eroded by three core tradeoffs: (1) local information advantages versus rent-seeking costs; (2) locally-defined notions of deprivation versus elite capture and non-pro-poor local preferences; (3) strategic targeting by communities in response to external funding criteria. Evidence for CBT is mixed and largely correlational. CBT performance depends heavily on community social structure, the accountability environment, and whether local agents are embedded in community affairs or captured by narrow interests.
gap_addressed: The CBT literature lacked a systematic theoretical framework for evaluating when and why community targeting outperforms centralised methods. Existing evidence was largely descriptive and could not support causal claims.
gaps_highlighted: Very little rigorous empirical evidence from Sub-Saharan Africa. The political economy of targeting committee decisions is undertheorised. The conditions under which local information advantages outweigh rent-seeking costs remain unclear. The paper does not address contexts of near-universal poverty where the premise of meaningful local discrimination breaks down.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: Conning and Kevane's principal-agent framework is the theoretical backbone for the Mali committee dilemma: local committees are agents with private information but operating under incentives the principal (program designers) cannot observe. In Mali's Sahel, their third tradeoff — strategic community responses to external criteria — is especially acute: when selection ratios are so low relative to actual vulnerability, the 'strategic targeting' dynamic becomes socially explosive rather than merely inefficient.

title: Monitoring Elite Capture in Community-Driven Development
methodology: Theoretical analysis combined with comparative case study evidence from Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. Develops a conceptual framework for when elite capture is likely to occur in community-driven development (CDD) programs, analyses why participatory spaces often intensify rather than reduce elite opportunism, and derives conditions under which monitoring and coordination mechanisms can mitigate capture.
data_used: No primary data collected. Draws on secondary case study evidence from CDD programs across Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, supplemented by the broader participatory development literature.
key_findings: Elite capture is endemic in CDD programs because elites possess superior skills to interact with external resources and speak the technical language of development agencies, making their gatekeeping role appear legitimate to wider communities. Newly-created participatory spaces do not mitigate elite opportunism — they actively provide elites with new incentives to intervene. Fraud detection mechanisms are necessary but insufficient; coordination mechanisms among aid agencies are also required to tame opportunism. Crucially, the standard aid incentive structure — punishing failures, rewarding successes — systematically worsens the problem by discouraging agencies from confronting capture.
gap_addressed: The CDD literature was overly optimistic about community self-governance and lacked systematic analysis of the mechanisms through which elites capture participatory programs and what institutional responses can work.
gaps_highlighted: How to build effective monitoring systems in weak-state and conflict-affected contexts. Whether vertical accountability (to NGOs or the state) can substitute for absent horizontal accountability within communities. Long-term sustainability of anti-capture mechanisms. The paper does not address contexts where poverty is so widespread that the elite/non-elite consumption distinction collapses — changing the nature of the political economy problem entirely.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: Platteau's framework is essential background for the Mali committee dilemma, but it requires inversion: in Mali's near-universal poverty context, the threat to targeting committees is not elite capture from above but lateral social pressure from a homogeneous poor population. When everyone qualifies on welfare grounds, committees face impossible demands from all sides simultaneously — a different and arguably more intractable problem than the clientelism Platteau diagnoses.

title: Do Local Officials Know Something We Don't? Decentralization of Targeted Transfers in Albania
methodology: Econometric analysis comparing consumption-predicted eligibility with actual local targeting decisions under Albania's ndihme ekonomike (social assistance) program. Regressions test whether commune-level targeting decisions incorporate poverty-relevant information beyond what proxy indicators from a standard household survey can capture. The residual targeting accuracy — i.e. what is explained over and above the statistical model — is attributed to local informational advantage.
data_used: Albania Living Standards Measurement Survey (August–November 1996), a multi-purpose modular household survey covering consumption expenditures, assets, and demographics. Merged with administrative data on social assistance allocations. Sample covers urban and rural communes across Albania, with approximately 1,500 households.
key_findings: Albania's social assistance program is relatively well-targeted by the standards of low-income country safety nets. Critically, targeting accuracy exceeds what proxy indicators alone would predict — communes appear to use local information that a questionnaire or statistical formula cannot capture. This informational advantage persists even after controlling for the level of central government funding allocated to each commune, suggesting genuine local knowledge rather than mechanical formula application.
gap_addressed: Whether decentralising targeting decisions to local officials actually improves outcomes relative to centralised formula-based approaches. The paper provides one of the earliest empirical tests of the 'local information advantage' premise that underlies CBT advocacy.
gaps_highlighted: Results are specific to Albania's post-communist transition context and may not generalise to lower-capacity states with weaker administrative traditions. The study cannot cleanly separate local informational advantages from political bias in allocation decisions. It does not test whether local information advantages erode under high poverty density, nor whether the benefits of local knowledge persist over time or across program expansions.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: Alderman provides the empirical foundation for the argument that local agents know who is poor — a premise CBT depends on. In Mali's Sahel, this premise holds in a perverse form: committees know perfectly well that nearly everyone is vulnerable. The problem is not informational but structural — local knowledge cannot support legitimate discrimination when selection ratios are so low relative to actual need that targeting criteria become socially incoherent.

title: Targeting Outcomes Redux
authors: David Coady, Margaret Grosh, John Hoddinott
methodology: Meta-analysis of 122 targeted antipoverty interventions across 48 developing countries. Constructs a standardised 'targeting differential' — the ratio of the share of benefits received by the poor to their population share — allowing cross-program comparison of targeting performance. Cross-program regressions identify country and program characteristics associated with better targeting outcomes.
data_used: A newly constructed database of 122 program evaluations spanning 48 countries, drawing on household survey data, administrative records, and program documentation. Programs include conditional and unconditional cash transfers, food subsidies, public works, and geographic targeting schemes.
key_findings: The median program transfers approximately 25% more to poor individuals than a universal allocation would — a positive but modest targeting differential. However, roughly a quarter of programs are regressive. Targeting performance is better in richer, more accountable countries and where inequality is higher. Means testing, geographic targeting, and workfare self-selection are all associated with higher shares of benefits reaching the poor. CBT is not singled out as a consistently superior method and shows high variance in outcomes. More administrative spending on targeting is associated with improved accuracy.
gap_addressed: Systematic cross-program evidence on the efficacy of targeting mechanisms was lacking. Debates about whether to target — and how — were conducted without a comprehensive empirical baseline on what targeting actually achieves in practice.
gaps_highlighted: Evidence from Sub-Saharan Africa is thin. The analysis cannot account for program context or implementation quality. The targeting differential metric itself assumes a meaningful poor/non-poor distinction — it breaks down conceptually in near-universal poverty settings where almost the entire population qualifies. Cost-effectiveness of different targeting mechanisms is not assessed. Long-run political economy effects of targeting choices are not examined.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: The paper's central metric — the targeting differential — becomes analytically degenerate in Mali's Sahel: when poverty rates approach universality, any selection scheme converges to random allocation and the differential collapses toward 1. This is precisely the 'managing the impossible' dynamic; Coady et al.'s framework, designed for contexts with a meaningful non-poor population to exclude, cannot capture the structural impossibility facing Malian committees.

title: Reaching the Poor: Cash Transfer Program Targeting in Cameroon
authors: Quentin Stoeffler, Bradford Mills, Carlo del Ninno
methodology: Ex-post performance assessment of PMT and CBT using data from Cameroon's Social Safety Nets Pilot Project (SSNPP), which employed a hybrid targeting design — PMT and CBT were conducted independently, allowing direct comparison of the two lists against a common welfare benchmark. Multiple targeting performance indicators are used, including inclusion and exclusion error rates measured against per-capita consumption and alternative poverty definitions.
data_used: Household survey data from Cameroon's SSNPP, covering approximately 65,000 rural and urban households across five regions of Cameroon, including the Far North, North, Adamawa, East, and cities of Douala and Yaoundé. Data includes consumption expenditures, assets, and demographic characteristics.
key_findings: CBT performs poorly relative to PMT in identifying households with low per-capita consumption. CBT tends to select households with low physical and human capital regardless of actual consumption levels, suggesting committees use proxy criteria that diverge from consumption-based poverty. CBT also shows higher variance in selection decisions than PMT. The results counsel caution in deploying CBT in environments characterised by high poverty rates and limited administrative capacity. Better integration of PMT and CBT, with explicit and monitorable selection criteria, could improve CBT performance.
gap_addressed: Rigorous comparative evidence on PMT vs. CBT performance in a Sub-Saharan African context was scarce. The hybrid design of Cameroon's SSNPP provided a rare opportunity to evaluate both mechanisms against the same households and welfare benchmark.
gaps_highlighted: The study does not fully explain the mechanisms behind CBT underperformance — whether it reflects poverty density, social dynamics within communities, or implementation quality. It does not test whether CBT performance improves with better-designed criteria or stronger monitoring. The findings may be specific to Cameroon's northern regions where poverty is concentrated and governance is weak.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: This is the closest empirical analogue to the Mali context in the CBT literature: a Sahelian-adjacent, high-poverty, low-capacity setting where CBT demonstrably fails to identify the consumption-poorest. The 'caution in high-poverty environments' finding speaks directly to the committee dilemma — when nearly everyone is poor, community selection criteria drift from consumption toward observable proxies that committees can defend socially, but which do not reliably reach the poorest.

title: Targeting Food Security Interventions When 'Everyone is Poor': The Case of Ethiopia's Productive Safety Net Programme
authors: Sarah Coll-Black, Daniel O. Gilligan, John F. Hoddinott, Neha Kumar, Alemayehu Seyoum Taffesse, William Wiseman
methodology: Assessment of the PSNP's targeting performance using the Coady-Grosh-Hoddinott (CGH) targeting differential index, supplemented by analysis of household survey data. Examines whether the programme reaches households that are both food insecure and consumption-poor, and benchmarks targeting accuracy against a global database of safety net programs. Community-based beneficiary identification processes (woredas, kebele food security task forces) are analysed as the operational targeting mechanism.
data_used: PSNP household survey data from rural Ethiopia covering more than one million beneficiary households. Administrative data on beneficiary lists. Merged with consumption and food security measures from national household surveys. Benchmarked against the Coady, Grosh & Hoddinott (2004) global program database.
key_findings: The PSNP is well-targeted by international standards, scoring above the global median on the CGH targeting differential and outperforming all African safety net programs in the Coady et al. benchmark. The programme successfully reaches households that are simultaneously food insecure and consumption-poor. However, the 'everyone is poor' context means that community targeting operates under high social pressure and that fine-grained within-community discrimination remains difficult. Strong external accountability through woreda-level oversight is identified as a key factor in maintaining targeting quality.
gap_addressed: How social safety nets can achieve acceptable targeting accuracy in contexts of widespread poverty where within-community welfare variation is low. The paper directly confronts the theoretical challenge that underpins the CBT literature's implicit assumption of meaningful poor/non-poor distinctions.
gaps_highlighted: The mechanisms through which community-level targeting decisions are made — and distorted — under near-universal poverty are not fully unpacked. Sustainability of targeting accuracy over program cycles is not tested. The analysis does not address what happens when selection ratios are reduced further (i.e., when budget constraints tighten relative to the eligible population). Ethiopia's relatively strong woreda administration makes generalisation to lower-capacity Sahelian states uncertain.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: The title alone — 'everyone is poor' — encapsulates the central challenge of the Mali committee dilemma. The paper shows that even with strong external oversight, targeting under near-universal poverty is difficult; in Mali's Sahel, where state administrative capacity is far weaker than Ethiopia's and selection ratios are more extreme, the committee dilemma becomes fully intractable rather than merely challenging.

title: Targeting for Social Safety Nets: Evidence from Nine Programs in the Sahel
methodology: Harmonised comparative analysis of household survey data from nine social protection programs implemented across Sahel countries. PMT and CBT selection lists are compared against two welfare benchmarks — per-capita consumption and food security — using multiple targeting performance indexes. The analysis evaluates programs as actually implemented (including prior geographic targeting) rather than relying on simulations, allowing real-world performance assessment. Coverage rates across programs ranged from 0.4% to 1.6% of national populations.
data_used: Household survey data from nine social protection programs across Sahel countries (Burkina Faso, Chad, Mali, Mauritania, Niger, Senegal). Data were harmonised across programs for comparability. Funded by the World Bank Sahel Adaptive Social Protection Program. Published as World Bank Policy Research Working Paper 9816 (2021); later as Journal of Development Studies 60(4):574-595 (2024).
key_findings: PMT outperforms CBT in identifying households with the lowest per-capita consumption. However, when food security is used as the welfare metric, or when distribution-sensitive targeting measures are applied, PMT and CBT perform similarly — and both differ little from random or universal allocation. Targeting performance depends critically on which welfare objective is being pursued, what share of households is selected, and which index is used to evaluate outcomes. Targeting costs represent a small fraction of total program budgets.
gap_addressed: Most CBT vs. PMT comparisons rely on simulations using survey data rather than evaluating programs as actually implemented. This paper fills that gap with real-world Sahel program data, capturing the full implementation context including geographic pre-targeting and administrative constraints.
gaps_highlighted: The political economy of why communities and administrators make the targeting decisions they do is not examined. The paper does not investigate how targeting accuracy changes as selection ratios are reduced further. It cannot identify which program design features drive performance differences across the nine programs. The convergence toward random allocation at very low coverage rates is documented but not theorised.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: This is the most direct empirical evidence for the 'managing the impossible' dynamic in the Sahel: at the coverage rates typical of these programs (under 2%), both PMT and CBT converge to near-random allocation — exactly the structural incoherence the Mali paper diagnoses. The finding that targeting method barely matters when selection ratios are this low reframes the committee dilemma as a budget and coverage problem, not an information or governance problem.

title: Multi-component Cash Transfer Programs: Evidence from Mali's Social Safety Net Program (Jigisémèjiri)
authors: Melissa Hidrobo, Lieven Huybregts, Naureen Karachiwalla, Shalini Roy
methodology: Impact evaluation using a two-stage randomised control trial. Ninety-six communes across five regions of Mali were randomly assigned to early (2014–2015) or late (2016–2017) treatment. Baseline survey conducted September 2014–February 2015. Follow-up surveys assessed impacts of three program components: cash transfers, accompanying measures (behaviour change communication), and a preventive nutrition package (PNP). Conducted jointly by IFPRI and the Institut de Recherche pour le Développement (IRD).
data_used: Household panel survey data from 96 communes in five southern regions of Mali (Sikasso, Koulikoro, Kayes, Mopti, Ségou). Households received 30,000 FCFA every three months. Outcomes measured include consumption, savings, productive assets, child anthropometry (length-for-age z-scores), anaemia prevalence, dietary diversity, and women's empowerment indicators.
key_findings: The program significantly increased savings and productive asset values among early-treatment households, particularly livestock and transport assets. The nutrition package reduced severe anaemia and improved children's length-for-age z-scores, indicating reductions in chronic undernutrition. However, dietary diversity and women's empowerment showed limited improvement. The multi-component design outperformed cash-only transfers on nutritional outcomes. Recommendations include increasing transfer frequency and strengthening the gender-transformative dimensions of accompanying measures.
gap_addressed: Evidence on whether integrating cash transfers with nutrition packages and behaviour change communication achieves better outcomes than cash alone in Mali's high-poverty Sahelian context. Also contributes to the evidence base on multi-component safety net design in fragile settings.
gaps_highlighted: The paper focuses on program impacts rather than targeting quality — the community committee selection process is not analysed. It does not address how committees managed beneficiary selection under near-universal poverty or what targeting errors occurred. Effects on women's empowerment and dietary diversity are weak, raising questions about accompanying measure design. The study covers only southern regions; conflict-affected northern Mali is excluded.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: This is the primary empirical reference for Mali's Jigisémèjiri program — the exact program context in which the Mali CBT paper is situated. While the paper is silent on targeting mechanics, it documents the community committee infrastructure used for selection and the extreme poverty context. The gaps it leaves — on how committees navigated selection under near-universal vulnerability — are precisely the research space the Mali paper occupies.

title: Transformative Social Protection
methodology: Conceptual framework development. The paper critically reviews the dominant World Bank Social Risk Management (SRM) framework and constructs an expanded typology of social protection measures across four functions: protective, preventive, promotive, and transformative. Illustrated with applied examples from Sub-Saharan Africa, particularly Uganda. No primary data collected.
data_used: Conceptual paper drawing on secondary literature and illustrative program examples from Sub-Saharan Africa. Applied to Uganda as a case context. Engages critically with World Bank policy documents and the SRM framework.
key_findings: The dominant economic 'safety net' framing of social protection is insufficient. The paper introduces a four-function typology — protective (relief from deprivation), preventive (risk reduction), promotive (livelihood enhancement), and transformative (addressing structural inequalities and social injustice). Transformative social protection challenges the social norms, power relations, and institutional structures that produce chronic vulnerability. CBT mechanisms, if unreformed, risk reproducing rather than challenging existing social hierarchies. Social as well as economic goals must be explicit in anti-poverty programming.
gap_addressed: The World Bank's SRM framework reduced social protection to economic risk management, neglecting its potential to challenge structural drivers of poverty. The paper fills the conceptual gap by articulating what a socially transformative approach to social protection would require.
gaps_highlighted: How to operationalise transformative social protection in practice remains underspecified. Measurement frameworks for transformative outcomes are underdeveloped. The political economy of transformative programming — why donors and governments resist it — is acknowledged but not fully analysed. The paper does not address the specific implementation challenges of targeting in near-universal poverty environments.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: The transformative framework reframes the Mali committee dilemma as a social justice problem, not merely a technical targeting failure. When near-universal poverty reflects structural inequalities — land access, gender, ethnicity, conflict exposure — criteria-based targeting that selects a fraction of the vulnerable population cannot be transformative. Devereux and Sabates-Wheeler's framework implies that the 'managing the impossible' dynamic in Mali is a symptom of insufficient program coverage, not a fixable implementation problem.

title: Realizing the Full Potential of Social Safety Nets in Africa
authors: Kathleen Beegle, Aline Coudouel, Emma Monsalve (eds.)
methodology: Systematic review and synthesis of evidence on social safety net programs across Sub-Saharan Africa. Draws on program evaluations, administrative data, household surveys, and political economy analyses from across the region. Organised around three cross-cutting themes: political dimensions, institutional foundations, and fiscal sustainability. Not a single-study empirical paper but a comprehensive policy research volume.
data_used: Multi-country evidence base drawn from safety net program evaluations, national household surveys, administrative program data, and World Bank country diagnostics across Sub-Saharan Africa. Covers targeting, delivery systems, program impacts, and systems-level governance across all SSA sub-regions including the Sahel.
key_findings: Safety net programs have expanded rapidly since the mid-2000s but coverage remains limited, particularly in the Sahel. Technical program design alone is insufficient — political economy, institutional anchoring in laws and policies, and fiscal sustainability are equally critical determinants of success. Targeting mechanisms (CBT and PMT) both face serious challenges in high-poverty African contexts. Scaling up requires shifting from project-mode to system-mode thinking, with strong interministerial coordination and domestic revenue mobilisation.
gap_addressed: The literature on SSA safety nets had focused heavily on technical design and impact evaluation, neglecting the political, institutional, and fiscal constraints that prevent technically sound programs from reaching scale. This volume fills that gap with a comprehensive systems perspective.
gaps_highlighted: Targeting under conditions of near-universal poverty is identified as an unresolved challenge but not fully theorised. The specific dynamics of community-based targeting committees — and the social pressures they face — are not analysed. Fragile and conflict-affected states, including Mali, receive limited dedicated treatment. The tension between targeting precision and coverage breadth is acknowledged but not resolved.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: The book's central argument — that political economy and institutional capacity, not just targeting methodology, determine safety net performance — is foundational for the Mali case. In Mali's Sahel, the committee dilemma is inseparable from weak state capacity, contested local authority, and the political impossibility of excluding near-universally poor neighbours. Beegle et al. provide the macro frame within which the micro-level 'managing the impossible' dynamic must be situated.

title: The Provision of Incentives in Firms
methodology: Comprehensive literature survey synthesising theoretical and empirical work on incentive provision in employment relationships. Reviews principal-agent models of piece rates, promotions, discretionary bonuses, deferred compensation, and profit sharing. Evaluates whether empirical evidence supports theoretical predictions and identifies gaps. No primary data collected.
data_used: No primary data. Draws on a large body of theoretical principal-agent literature and empirical studies of compensation contracts across industries and institutional settings.
key_findings: Individuals respond to pay-for-performance, but firms often do not design contracts as agency theory predicts. Output measurement problems are the central constraint on incentive provision — the harder it is to measure performance, the weaker the incentive contract. The Informativeness Principle holds that any signal informative about agent performance should enter the compensation contract. Relative performance evaluation (comparing agents against each other) is theoretically optimal when common shocks are present. Workers with multi-dimensional, hard-to-measure tasks receive systematically weaker incentives, which distorts effort allocation toward measurable dimensions.
gap_addressed: The principal-agent literature had accumulated many competing theoretical models without systematic assessment of their empirical validity. The paper fills this gap by evaluating both what theory predicts and what empirical evidence actually shows about contract design.
gaps_highlighted: Little progress distinguishing among competing theoretical mechanisms. The paper acknowledges that how incentives are provided to workers whose output is difficult to measure — the classic public sector problem — remains poorly understood. Empirical identification of incentive effects in field settings is weak. Application to non-firm settings (NGOs, government programs, community committees) is not developed.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: Prendergast's framework maps directly onto the Mali committee dilemma. Targeting committee members are agents whose output — accurate poverty identification — is nearly impossible to measure and verify. Under near-universal poverty, even the Informativeness Principle breaks down: available poverty proxies become uninformative relative to the selection threshold, leaving committees without actionable criteria and program designers without any contractable performance signal.

title: Incentives and Organizations in the Public Sector: An Interpretative Review
methodology: Interpretative literature review applying principal-agent theory to public sector organisations. Classifies public sector principal-agent relationships into three categories based on information flow. Reviews theoretical extensions covering multitask, multiperiod, and multiprincipal contexts. Draws on empirical case studies of public sector incentive schemes to assess how well theory maps onto practice. Derives implications for organisational reform and design.
data_used: No primary data. Draws on the theoretical principal-agent literature and empirical case studies of incentive schemes in public sector organisations across multiple country contexts.
key_findings: Low-powered incentives are more appropriate in the public sector than the private sector due to: (1) multitasking — agents must pursue multiple objectives simultaneously; (2) multiple principals — public sector agents answer to political supervisors, managers, and clients with conflicting objectives; (3) hard-to-measure outputs — performance metrics are difficult to define and verify; (4) intrinsic motivation — public sector workers often have mission-driven preferences that high-powered incentives can crowd out. Applying private sector performance incentives to public sector contexts produces predictable dysfunctions.
gap_addressed: Principal-agent theory developed primarily in private firm contexts was being applied to public sector reform without adequate adjustment for the distinctive features of government agencies. The paper provides an analytically grounded bridge between the two literatures.
gaps_highlighted: Empirical research on public sector reform should assess success probability relative to specific organisational characteristics rather than seeking blanket verdicts. Intrinsic motivation in public sector workers remains undertheorised. The interaction between political oversight and frontline agent behaviour needs further development. The paper does not extend to non-governmental agents (NGOs, community committees) operating in hybrid principal structures.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: Dixit's multiprincipal framework is the most direct theoretical lens for the Mali committee dilemma. Targeting committee members answer simultaneously to program designers (select the poorest), local political authorities (maintain patronage), village social norms (treat neighbours fairly), and their own households (avoid retaliation). Under near-universal poverty, these competing principal demands become irreconcilable — the 'managing the impossible' dynamic is precisely a multiprincipal crisis with no feasible action that satisfies all principals at once.

title: Street-Level Bureaucracy: Dilemmas of the Individual in Public Services
methodology: Qualitative and ethnographic study of frontline public service workers — including teachers, police officers, social workers, and legal-aid lawyers — in the United States. Combines observation, case study analysis, and conceptual framework development. No quantitative primary data; draws on ethnographic field observations and secondary case material to build a general theory of street-level implementation. An expanded 30th anniversary edition was published in 2010.
data_used: Ethnographic observation and case studies of US public sector workers across multiple service domains. Secondary case material from a range of public service contexts. No survey or experimental data.
key_findings: Street-level bureaucrats (SLBs) — frontline workers who interact directly with the public — are de facto policymakers because their discretionary decisions constitute policy in practice. SLBs work under chronic resource inadequacy, ambiguous organisational goals, and impossible caseloads. Unable to serve all clients fully, they develop systematic coping mechanisms: rationing, routinising, screening clients for tractability, and simplifying case categories. These adaptations systematically disadvantage the most complex and vulnerable clients. Reform efforts that impose new performance metrics produce behavioural gaming rather than genuine improvement — SLBs satisfy measured targets while subverting unmeasured policy objectives. Structural reform of working conditions is required; discretion cannot be eliminated through administrative design.
gap_addressed: The implementation literature treated frontline workers as passive conduits of policy. Lipsky fills the gap by showing that the gap between policy intent and policy outcome is produced by the structural conditions under which SLBs work, not by individual incompetence or bad faith.
gaps_highlighted: The framework was developed in the US context and its generalisability to low-capacity, low-income country settings is untested. How to design accountability mechanisms that improve SLB behaviour without producing gaming is not resolved. The role of community-level (non-state) agents operating in hybrid authority structures is not addressed. Intrinsic motivation and its interaction with coping strategies is undertheorised.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: The Mali committee dilemma is a Lipskian scenario in its purest form. Targeting committee members are street-level bureaucrats with an impossible mandate — select a small fraction of a near-universally poor community using criteria that cannot bear the social weight placed on them. Lipsky predicts exactly what happens: committees develop coping mechanisms (rotation of beneficiaries, social criteria substitution, avoidance of hard cases) that systematically diverge from program intent. The 'managing the impossible' framing is borrowed directly from Lipsky's core thesis.

title: Policy Work: Street-Level Organizations Under New Managerialism
methodology: Comparative organisational analysis of US welfare agencies operating under New Public Management (NPM) reforms. Multi-site qualitative study tracing how performance-based management strategies reshape frontline discretionary practices. Draws on field observations, interviews with caseworkers and managers, and analysis of performance management systems. Published as part of a special symposium on street-level organisations in JPART.
data_used: Qualitative organisational data from US welfare and workfare agencies undergoing NPM reform. Caseworker observation data, semi-structured interviews, and documentary analysis of performance targets and management directives across multiple agency sites.
key_findings: Street-level organisations are pivotal policy-making actors, not merely implementers. NPM performance metrics do not eliminate discretion — they redirect it. Caseworkers make informal cost-benefit calculations: they select action A over B when A is less costly and more rewarding within the performance incentive structure. This produces 'performance games' where workers satisfy measurable targets while subverting unmeasured policy objectives. NPM standardisation suppresses client-centred responsiveness and personal relationships. Even under heavy managerial pressure, frontline workers defend and maintain discretionary space. Policies only become real through street-level action.
gap_addressed: Lipsky's 1980 framework was developed before NPM reforms restructured welfare bureaucracies. The paper updates SLB theory for the managerial reform era, showing that management by objectives and performance auditing creates new forms of discretion rather than resolving the classic SLB dilemma.
gaps_highlighted: Non-public actors — NGOs, community organisations, volunteer committees — operating in SLB-like roles are not fully theorised. The framework is developed in high-income country welfare states with relatively strong administrative capacity; application to low-capacity developing country contexts is not addressed. Digital transformation of casework and its interaction with discretion is emerging but undertheorised.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: Brodkin's central insight — that management interventions reshape rather than eliminate SLB discretion — maps directly onto the Mali committee dilemma. When program designers introduce PMT scores, eligibility criteria, or community monitoring to constrain committee decisions, Brodkin's framework predicts that committee members will adapt by gaming measurable criteria while maintaining the social accommodations that near-universal poverty demands. The 'managing the impossible' dynamic is not solved by adding oversight; it is displaced into new coping strategies.
